title: Troubleshooting Emotion Regulation Skills: When What You Are Doing Isn’t Working 1
source_marker: Emotion Regulation Handout 24   (Emotion Regulation Worksheet 16) (p. 1 of 2)

pages.text:
266
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Emotion Regulation Handout 24   (Emotion Regulation Worksheet 16) (p. 1 of 2)
Troubleshooting Emotion Regulation Skills: When What
You Are Doing Isn’t Working
1
Check Your Biological Sensitivity
•• Ask: Am I biologically more vulnerable?
Do I have untreated physical illness or distress?
Am I out of balance on eating, use of drugs, sleep, exercise?
Have I taken medications as prescribed?
•• Work on your PLEASE skills.
1.	 Take care of physical illness and distress.
2.	 Take medications as prescribed. Check if others are needed.
3.	 Try again.
2
Check Your Skills
•• Review what you have tried.
Did you try a skill likely to be effective?
Did you follow the skill instructions to the letter?
•• Work on your skills.
1.	 Review and try other skills.
2.	 Get coaching if you need it.
3.	 Try again.
3
Check for Reinforcers
•• Ask: Do my emotions . . .
Communicate an important message or influence people to do things?
Motivate me to do things I think are important?
Validate my beliefs or my identity?
Feel good?
•• If yes:
1.	 Practice interpersonal effectiveness skills to communicate.
2.	 Work to find new reinforcers to motivate yourself.
3.	 Practice self-validation.
4.	 Do pros and cons for changing emotions.
(See Emotion Regulation Worksheet 1.)
(continued on next page)

267
Emotion Regulation Handout 24   (p. 2 of 2)
4
Check Your Mood
•• Ask: Am I putting in the time and effort that solving my problem will take?
•• If no:
1.	 Do pros and cons for working hard on skills.
2.	 Practice radical acceptance and willingness skills.
3.	 Practice the mindfulness skills of participating and effectiveness
(See Mindfulness Handouts 4 and 5.)
5
Check for Emotional Overload
•• Ask: Am I too upset to use complicated skills?
•• If yes, ask: Can the problems I am worrying about be easily solved now?
•• If yes, do problem solving.
(See Emotion Regulation Handouts 9, 12.)
•• If no, practice mindfulness of current emotions.
(See Emotion Regulation Handout 22.)
•• If your emotions are too high for you to think straight:
•• Go to TIP skills.
(See Distress Tolerance Handout 5.)
6
Check for Emotion Myths Getting in the Way
•• Check for:
Judgmental myths about emotions (e.g., “Some emotions are stupid,” “There is a
right way to feel in every situation”)?
Beliefs that emotions and identity are the same (e.g., “My emotions are who I
am”)?
•• If yes:
1.	 Check the facts.
2.	 Challenge myths.
3.	 Practice thinking nonjudgmentally.

full_text:
Emotion Regulation Handout 24   (Emotion Regulation Worksheet 16) (p. 1 of 2)
Troubleshooting Emotion Regulation Skills: When What
You Are Doing Isn’t Working
Check Your Biological Sensitivity
•• Ask: Am I biologically more vulnerable?
Do I have untreated physical illness or distress?
Am I out of balance on eating, use of drugs, sleep, exercise?
Have I taken medications as prescribed?
•• Work on your PLEASE skills.
1.	 Take care of physical illness and distress.
2.	 Take medications as prescribed. Check if others are needed.
3.	 Try again.
Check Your Skills
•• Review what you have tried.
Did you try a skill likely to be effective?
Did you follow the skill instructions to the letter?
•• Work on your skills.
1.	 Review and try other skills.
2.	 Get coaching if you need it.
3.	 Try again.
Check for Reinforcers
•• Ask: Do my emotions . . .
Communicate an important message or influence people to do things?
Motivate me to do things I think are important?
Validate my beliefs or my identity?
Feel good?
•• If yes:
1.	 Practice interpersonal effectiveness skills to communicate.
2.	 Work to find new reinforcers to motivate yourself.
3.	 Practice self-validation.
4.	 Do pros and cons for changing emotions.
(See Emotion Regulation Worksheet 1.)
(continued on next page)

Emotion Regulation Handout 24   (p. 2 of 2)
Check Your Mood
•• Ask: Am I putting in the time and effort that solving my problem will take?
•• If no:
1.	 Do pros and cons for working hard on skills.
2.	 Practice radical acceptance and willingness skills.
3.	 Practice the mindfulness skills of participating and effectiveness
(See Mindfulness Handouts 4 and 5.)
Check for Emotional Overload
•• Ask: Am I too upset to use complicated skills?
•• If yes, ask: Can the problems I am worrying about be easily solved now?
•• If yes, do problem solving.
(See Emotion Regulation Handouts 9, 12.)
•• If no, practice mindfulness of current emotions.
(See Emotion Regulation Handout 22.)
•• If your emotions are too high for you to think straight:
•• Go to TIP skills.
(See Distress Tolerance Handout 5.)
Check for Emotion Myths Getting in the Way
•• Check for:
Judgmental myths about emotions (e.g., “Some emotions are stupid,” “There is a
right way to feel in every situation”)?
Beliefs that emotions and identity are the same (e.g., “My emotions are who I
am”)?
•• If yes:
1.	 Check the facts.
2.	 Challenge myths.
3.	 Practice thinking nonjudgmentally.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Troubleshooting Emotion Regulation Skills: When What You Are Doing Isn’t Working 1
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Emotion work starts with the body and access needs. Hunger, pain, oxygen, sleep attacks, MCAS reactions, trauma cues, and exhaustion count.
tiny_steps: Check body basics: breathing, pain, food/water, meds, temperature, sensory load.
Lower the demand by one size.
Choose soothe, solve, pause, or ask for help.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Allow anger and grief without turning them into shame.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not demand immediate calm during respiratory distress, pain flares, dissociation, or overload.
